materials.extractedText:
1. The Role of Established Design Patterns in Modern Web Development (17 Marks) 
Established design patterns provide standardized, reusable solutions to common 
software design problems. In modern web app development, they act as blueprints that 
developers can customize to solve recurring architectural, structural, or behavioral 
challenges. By providing a shared vocabulary and proven methodologies, design 
patterns signiﬁcantly enhance a web application's robustness and adaptability. 
Analysis of Speciﬁc Patterns 
 
Shutterstock 
Model-View-Controller (MVC) Pattern 
The MVC pattern divides an application into three interconnected components: 
 
Model: Manages the data, logic, and rules of the application. 
 
View: Handles the user interface and data presentation. 
 
Controller: Accepts input, manipulates the model, and updates the view. 
 
Beneﬁts: MVC drastically improves maintainability by enforcing a separation of 
concerns. A front-end developer can modify the View without impacting the 
underlying Model logic. It also aids scalability, as components can be tested 
and updated independently. 
Responsive Design Pattern 
While often categorized as a UI approach, Responsive Design functions as a structural 
pattern for modern web interfaces. It dictates that a website should ﬂuidly adapt to the 
user's device.
 
Beneﬁts: This pattern primarily drives user experience (UX). Instead of 
maintaining separate mobile and desktop sites, a single codebase dynamically 
adjusts. This improves maintainability and ensures a consistent, accessible 
experience across all platforms. 
Singleton Pattern 
A creational design pattern that restricts the instantiation of a class to a single object. 
 
Example: Managing a database connection pool or an application's global 
conﬁguration settings. 
 
Beneﬁts: It prevents multiple, resource-heavy connections from being created 
simultaneously. This improves scalability by optimizing memory usage and 
ensures application robustness by providing a strict, globally accessible point of 
access to a shared resource. 
Conclusion on Robustness: By leveraging patterns like MVC for structure, Singleton for 
resource management, and Responsive Design for presentation, developers build web 
applications that can easily absorb new features, handle increased traƯic, and adapt to 
shifting user behaviors without requiring complete rewrites. 
 
2. The Lifecycle of a Web Application: An E-commerce Scenario (16 Marks) 
The web application lifecycle is a structured methodology detailing the creation and 
ongoing management of software. Utilizing an e-commerce platform as an example, the 
stages are as follows: 
 
Shutterstock
Lifecycle Stages and Design Principles 
1. Initial Design (UI/UX and Architecture) 
 
Process: The team maps out the user journey for the e-commerce site, from 
browsing a catalog to the checkout process. Wireframes and system 
architectures are designed. 
 
Principles Leveraged: User-Centered Design and Separation of Concerns. The 
focus is on ensuring the user journey is intuitive, minimizing friction during the 
purchase process. 
2. Development 
 
Process: Coders write the application. The front-end team builds the product 
gallery, while the back-end team constructs the payment gateway. 
 
Principles Leveraged: Component-Based Architecture. Developers use 
versioning (via tools like Git) to manage changes to the codebase safely, 
ensuring multiple developers can work simultaneously without overwriting each 
other's code. 
3. Testing 
 
Process: The application is rigorously evaluated. The QA team attempts to break 
the shopping cart logic or input invalid credit card numbers. 
 
Principles Leveraged: Test-Driven Development. A critical component here is 
regression testing, which ensures that a newly added feature (like a discount 
code module) does not break existing, functioning parts of the application (like 
the basic checkout ﬂow). 
4. Deployment 
 
Process: The e-commerce site is pushed to live servers and made accessible to 
the public. 
 
Principles Leveraged: Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) 
design, enabling automated, zero-downtime releases. 
5. Maintenance 
 
Process: Post-launch, the team monitors server health, patches security 
vulnerabilities, and analyzes user behavior. 
 
Principles Leveraged: The principle of continuous iteration. Based on user 
feedback (e.g., users abandoning carts due to hidden shipping fees), the team 
iterates on the design, releasing updated versions of the application to 
continuously reﬁne the successful user journey.
3. Evaluating Architectural Patterns: Microservices vs. Serverless (16 Marks) 
Modern web applications often move away from traditional monolithic architectures to 
improve agility. Two prominent patterns are Microservices and Serverless. 
Advantages, Disadvantages, and TradeoƯs 
Feature
Microservices Architecture
Serverless Architecture
Concept 
App broken into small, 
independent services 
communicating via APIs. 
Infrastructure managed entirely by 
the cloud provider; code runs on 
demand. 
Scalability 
High. Individual services (e.g., just 
the search function) can be 
scaled independently. 
Extremely High. Autoscales 
instantly from zero to thousands of 
requests. 
Cost 
Fixed server costs persist even 
during low traƯic, but predictable 
at scale. 
Pay-as-you-go. Highly cost-
eƯective for irregular traƯic, but 
can become expensive under 
heavy, constant load. 
Operational 
Complexity 
Very High. Requires complex 
orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes), 
monitoring, and network 
management. 
Low for infrastructure, but High for 
debugging distributed code logic 
and handling "cold starts." 
Three Distinct Examples 
1. Netﬂix (Microservices Example): Netﬂix uses microservices to separate its 
billing system from its video recommendation engine. TradeoƯ: High operational 
complexity managed by dedicated engineering teams, justiﬁed by the need for 
ultimate scalability and fault tolerance (if recommendations fail, streaming still 
works). 
2. Image Processing App (Serverless Example): An app that allows users to 
upload avatars. Using AWS Lambda (Serverless), the image resizing function only 
runs—and costs money—when an image is actively uploaded. TradeoƯ: Loss of 
control over the underlying server environment, but massive savings on idle 
infrastructure costs. 
3. E-commerce Checkout (Microservices Example): An inventory microservice 
checks stock levels, while a separate payment microservice processes the card.
Challenge: Ensuring data consistency across independent databases requires 
complex distributed transaction management. 
 
4. Accessibility by Design in Web App Development (16 Marks) 
"Accessibility by Design" is the philosophy of integrating accessibility considerations 
into the very ﬁrst stages of the software development lifecycle, rather than treating it as 
an afterthought or a post-launch patch. This "shift-left" approach ensures that 
applications are fundamentally inclusive and usable by individuals with visual, auditory, 
motor, or cognitive disabilities. 
Early Stage Incorporation & Practical Strategies 
Incorporating accessibility early means wireframes are evaluated for color contrast 
before coding begins, and technical architectures are chosen based on their ability to 
support assistive technologies. 
Practical strategies for adhering to Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 
include: 
 
Implementing Semantic HTML: This is the most critical technical strategy. 
Using semantic HTML means choosing the correct HTML elements for their 
intended purpose (e.g., using <nav> for navigation, <button> for clickable 
actions, and <h1> to <h6> for a logical document outline) rather than generic 
<div> tags. Screen readers rely heavily on semantic HTML to convey the structure 
and meaning of a page to visually impaired users. 
 
Keyboard Navigation Support: Ensuring that all interactive elements (links, 
forms, dropdowns) can be accessed and operated using only the "Tab" and 
"Enter" keys, aiding users with motor disabilities who cannot use a mouse. 
 
Alternative Text for Media: Providing descriptive alt text for all informative 
images, allowing screen readers to describe the visual content to blind users. 
 
Color Contrast Ratios: Designing UI elements with suƯicient contrast between 
text and background colors (WCAG recommends a ratio of at least 4.5:1 for 
normal text) to assist users with low vision or color blindness. 
 
5. Front-End vs. Back-End Development (16 Marks) 
Web application development is broadly divided into two distinct but highly 
collaborative disciplines: front-end (client-side) and back-end (server-side) 
development.
Roles, Responsibilities, and Comparison 
Discipline Core Responsibilities 
Typical 
Technologies 
Focus Area 
Front-End
Translating design into code, 
managing user interface (UI), 
ensuring cross-browser 
compatibility, and optimizing 
user experience (UX). 
HTML, CSS, 
JavaScript, React, 
Angular, Vue. 
What the user sees, 
touches, and 
interacts with directly.
Back-End
Server conﬁguration, business 
logic execution, data security, 
database management, and 
authentication. 
Python, Java, 
Node.js, SQL, 
NoSQL, Ruby. 
The underlying 
engines, databases, 
and APIs that power 
the application 
behind the scenes. 
Collaboration and RESTful APIs 
Overall development eƯiciency relies heavily on the seamless collaboration between 
these two teams. If front-end developers build interfaces without understanding the 
data structures provided by the back-end, integration will fail. 
The bridge between these two disciplines is typically built using RESTful APIs 
(Representational State Transfer Application Programming Interfaces). 
 
Understanding RESTful APIs: A RESTful API is a standardized set of rules that 
allows the front-end to communicate with the back-end over HTTP. For instance, 
when a user clicks "Load Proﬁle" on the front-end, a GET request is sent to a 
speciﬁc back-end endpoint (e.g., /api/users/123). The back-end retrieves the 
data from the database, formats it securely into a standard format (usually 
JSON), and sends it back via the API. The front-end then parses this JSON and 
visually renders the user's proﬁle on the screen. Clear API documentation 
ensures both teams can work in parallel eƯiciently. 
 
6. The Impact of Responsive Web Design (16 Marks) 
Responsive web design is the practice of building websites that automatically adapt 
and format themselves to look and function optimally on any device, from a large 
desktop monitor to a small smartphone screen. 
Impact on Engagement and Conversion
 
User Engagement: A responsive site drastically lowers bounce rates. If a mobile 
user visits a desktop-only site, they must pinch-and-zoom to read text, leading to 
frustration and immediate abandonment. Responsive design keeps users 
engaged by providing readable text and easily tappable navigation. 
 
Conversion Rates: For e-commerce or lead-generation sites, responsive design 
directly impacts revenue. A seamless, mobile-optimized checkout process 
removes friction, increasing the likelihood that a user will complete a purchase 
on their phone. 
Speciﬁc Implementation Techniques 
1. Flexible Grids 
Instead of using rigid, absolute measurements (like pixels) for page elements, 
developers use relative units (like percentages). This ensures that a column taking up 
50% of the screen on a desktop will scale down proportionally to ﬁt the screen of a 
tablet, maintaining a cohesive responsive layout regardless of the physical size of the 
device. 
2. Media Queries 
Media queries are CSS techniques that apply diƯerent stylistic rules based on the 
characteristics of the device loading the page, most commonly the viewport width. 
 
Application: A developer might write a media query stating: "If the viewport 
width is less than 768 pixels (a typical mobile phone), stack the navigation menu 
vertically instead of displaying it horizontally." This technique allows for radical 
structural changes to the UI to guarantee a better user experience across varying 
screen constraints.
